title: Eigenvalues and the Singular Value Decomposition

elements.html:
A scalar $\lambda$ is an eigenvalue of $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ if there exists a nonzero vector $x$ such that

$$Ax = \lambda x$$

The vector $x$ is the corresponding eigenvector. The set of all eigenvalues is the spectrum $\sigma(A)$.

For symmetric $A = A^T$, all eigenvalues are real and the eigenvectors form an orthonormal basis:

$$A = Q \Lambda Q^T, \quad Q^T Q = I$$
The spectral theorem guarantees this decomposition for all real symmetric matrices
Trefethen and Bau, Numerical Linear Algebra, SIAM, 1997.

slides.notes: Start with the eigenvalue definition. Emphasize the spectral theorem as the key structural result for symmetric matrices.

elements.html:
Every matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ admits a factorization

$$A = U \Sigma V^T$$

where:
• $U \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ is orthogonal (left singular vectors)
• $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is orthogonal (right singular vectors)
• $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is diagonal with $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \cdots \geq 0$

The singular values satisfy $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i(A^T A)}$.
Unlike eigendecomposition, SVD exists for any matrix, even rectangular ones
The SVD was independently discovered by Beltrami (1873) and Jordan (1874).

slides.notes: Introduce the SVD. Stress that it works for any matrix, not just square or symmetric ones.

elements.html:
The Eckart-Young-Mirsky theorem states that the best rank-$k$ approximation to $A$ (in either the 2-norm or Frobenius norm) is given by the truncated SVD:

$$A_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i \, u_i \, v_i^T$$

The approximation error is:

$$\|A - A_k\|_2 = \sigma_{k+1}, \qquad \|A - A_k\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^2}$$

where $r = \operatorname{rank}(A)$.
This is the theoretical foundation for PCA, LSI, and matrix completion
Rapid singular value decay $\Rightarrow$ effective low rank
Eckart and Young, Psychometrika, 1936. Mirsky, Quart. J. Math., 1960.

slides.notes: The Eckart-Young theorem is one of the most important results in applied linear algebra. Mention applications in data science.

elements.html:
Full SVD (dense): $O(mn \min(m,n))$ via Golub-Kahan bidiagonalization

Truncated SVD (top $k$ singular values):
• Lanczos / Arnoldi iteration: $O(mnk)$
• Randomized SVD (Halko, Martinsson, Tropp 2011):

$$\tilde{U}, \tilde{\Sigma}, \tilde{V} = \text{rsvd}(A, k, p)$$

    1. Form $Y = A \Omega$ where $\Omega \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (k+p)}$ is random
    2. Compute QR: $Y = QR$
    3. Form $B = Q^T A \in \mathbb{R}^{(k+p) \times n}$
    4. Compute SVD of small matrix $B$

Cost: $O(mn(k+p))$ with only two passes over $A$.
The oversampling parameter $p \approx 5{\text{--}}10$ suffices in practice
Halko, Martinsson, Tropp. Finding structure with randomness. SIAM Review, 2011.

slides.notes: Discuss practical algorithms. The randomized SVD is the workhorse for large-scale problems. Mention that scikit-learn uses it internally.
